segments.s250-g129: This is an indefinite noun phrase introducing the object: 'یکی' = 'a certain / one', followed by the noun 'مهر پیروزه' = 'turquoise seal'. 'رستم' stands as a modifying name (’Rostam’s’ / ‘of Rostam’) attached to that seal; the final token 'بروی' here functions as a terse poetic locator/relator ('on/with/bearing') linking the seal to what follows. Word order is noun phrase + modifier + locator, a compact, poetic phrasing.
segments.s250-g130: 'نبشته' is an adjectival/participial phrase meaning 'inscribed / written (on)', and it governs the following prepositional phrase 'به آهن' = 'on/into iron' (i.e. engraved in metal). 'بکردار موی' is a terse apposition: 'موی' = 'a lock of hair' and 'بکردار' qualifies it; the whole phrase says the inscribed seal is attached to / associated with a lock of hair. The sequence shows an object (the seal) described by its inscription and how it is fastened.
segments.s250-g131: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause ('when'). 'بار درخت وفا' is a genitive group 'the load/fruit of the tree of fidelity' (i.e. the result/produce of loyalty) and 'را' marks it as the definite object of the verb 'بدید' = 'he saw.' The order is typical Persian: temporal marker + object phrase + verb.
segments.s250-g132: 'بدانست' is the main verb meaning 'he knew / perceived.' The following 'کآمد' is the contracted poetic form of 'که آمد' ('that came' / 'what came') introducing the content of that knowing. 'غمش را کلید' is a nominal phrase 'the key of his grief' (with 'را' marking the definite object), so the clause means he realized that what had come was the key to his sorrow.
segments.s250-g133: 'بخندید' is the past tense verb 'he laughed.' The noun phrase 'خندیدنی شاهوار' follows as a descriptive nominal cluster: 'خندیدنی' ('laugh-worthy' / 'a laughter') qualified by 'شاهوار' ('regal/lofty'), giving a poetic characterization of the laugh. The verb plus postpositive descriptive phrase is a common poetic inversion: verb then manner/description.
segments.s250-g134: 'چنان' = 'thus/so' sets up the manner, then 'کآمد' ('it came') is the verb; 'آواز' (the voice/call) is the semantic subject/thing that appears, and 'بر چاهسار' = 'over/upon the well-field' is a locative phrase introduced by 'بر.' The clause literally says 'so came a voice upon the well-field.'
segments.s250-g135: 'منیژه' is the proper-name subject. 'چو' = 'when' introduces the temporal clause; 'بشنید' is the past tense verb 'she heard.' The noun 'خندیدنش' is a nominalized verb with the possessive clitic (-ش) = 'his/its laughing' and functions as the object of 'بشنید' ('she heard his/that laughter').
segments.s250-g136: This short phrase gives Manijeh's situation: 'ازان' = 'from that / in that', 'چاه تاریک' = 'dark well' (compound noun), 'بسته' = 'closed / bound', and 'تنش' = 'her body' (تن + the possessive clitic ش). The order is modifier-before-head for the compounds and the possessive clitic attaches to 'تن' to mark whose body it is.
segments.s250-g57: This clause is a reporting clause: the name منیژه is the subject, بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed immediately after the name (poetic word order), and گفت is the past-tense reporting verb 'said'. کز introduces what follows as a topical/relative prepositional link ('about/from'), and کار من is the noun phrase 'my affair(s)'.
segments.s250-g58: چه پرسی is an interrogative phrase where چه (what/why) combines with the present-second-person verb پرسی ('you ask' — here rendered as a general 'do you ask'). ز introduces the object/source ('about' or 'concerning'), followed by the coordinated nouns بدبخت و تیمار 'misfortune and care/sorrow' and the possessor من 'my' (placed after the nouns as Persian uses).
segments.s250-g59: This prepositional/locative phrase begins with کزان ('from that/whence'), then چاه سر 'the rim/top of the pit' (چاه = pit/well, سر = top). با دلی 'with a heart' uses با + an attributive form دلی; پر ز درد is a poetic way of saying 'full of pain' (پر = full, ز = from/of, درد = pain/sorrow).
segments.s250-g60: دویدم is the first-person past 'I ran/ran quickly'; بنزد is a fused prepositional form meaning 'to the presence of / toward' (poetic instead of به نزد). تو‌ای is a vocative inversion ('O you'/'thou'), and رادمرد is the vocative epithet 'noble man' or 'man of nobility' addressing the addressee.
segments.s250-g61: زدی is the past-second-person verb 'you gave/uttered'; here it takes بانگ 'a shout/cry' as its object. بر من marks the target 'against me', and چو جنگاوران is a simile 'like warriors' (چو = like/as). Word order places the emphasis on the action and the comparison.
segments.s250-g62: نترسیدی is the negative past-second-person 'you were not afraid' (ن- prefix + ترسیدی). از introduces the object of fear ('of/from'), here intensified by the tautological pair داور داوران literally 'judge of judges' (a poetic intensifier meaning the supreme judge/arbiter).
segments.s250-g63: This is a simple identification: منیژه (name) followed by منم 'I am' (colloquial/poetic contraction of منَم). دخت افراسیاب is a genitive construction 'daughter of Afrasiab' with دخت (daughter/maiden) before the father's name.
segments.s250-g64: This clause is a rhetorical question built with the negative-past verb ندیدی 'you did not see'. برهنه 'naked/exposed' modifies the following noun phrase رخم آفتاب: رخم is the possessive form 'my face' (رخ + ـم), and آفتاب ('the sun') here indicates exposure/influence — together giving 'my face exposed to the sun' (poetic word order places the adjective and verb before the noun phrase).
segments.s250-g257: This is an adverbial + locative + noun phrase. بدلش (بدل + ـش) functions adverbially as “in return / instead (of that)”; اندرون is a locative adverb “within / inside”; بانگ is the head noun “sound / cry.” رویینه خم is a postpositive descriptive phrase modifying بانگ: رویینه “armoured, iron” qualifies خم “bend/arch” (a poetic image) so together they colour بانگ as a metallic/armoured clang. Word order here is adverb (بدلش) → place (اندرون) → head noun (بانگ) → descriptive phrase (رویینه خم).
segments.s250-g258: A relative clause explaining the preceding sound. که is the relative pronoun “that / which”; آید is present-tense “comes” (simple present/indicative, used here to report action); ز is the poetic preposition “from”; ره رخش is a prepositional phrase “the road / way of Rakhsh” (رخش = the steed); پولاد سم is the noun phrase “steel hoof” (پولاد 'steel' qualifying سم 'hoof') which serves as the semantic subject that “comes” along the road. The clause literally reads: “which comes from the way of Rakhsh — (the) steel hoof.”
segments.s250-g65: کنون is a temporal adverb ('now') placed at the clause start. دیده پرخون is a noun + adjectival compound meaning 'eyes blood-filled' (noun before adjective). دل پر ز درد uses the adjective پر followed by the poetic preposition ز ('full of') plus its complement درد; word order is 'دل (heart) + پر ز (full of) + درد (pain)'.
segments.s250-g66: ازین در بدان در is a paired prepositional expression 'from this door to that door' — ازین is the archaic written form of 'از این' ('from this'), and the repetition در ... در marks 'door ... door'. The initial ب is the prepositional element 'به/ب' ('to/into') attached separately here; دوان گردگرد is the verb + adverbial ('runs/rushes, all around'), with گردگرد an adverbial intensifier.
segments.s250-g67: همی is the epic/habitual marker here ('still/continually') and combines with the first-person verb فرازآورم ('I lift up / I raise'), putting the action in an ongoing present. نان کشکین is the direct object before the verb: 'meagre/poor bread'.
segments.s250-g68: چنین is an adverb ('thus/so') modifying the clause. راند is a past/perfect verb 'drove/has driven'; its subject is the following noun phrase یزدان قضا ('God's decree' / 'divine fate'), and بر سرم ('upon my head') is the prepositional phrase indicating the target of that action. Note the somewhat inverted, poetic word order where cause/agent follows the verb.
segments.s250-g69: ازین زارتر is a comparative phrase 'more wretched than this' where از (here functioning as the comparative 'than') attaches to ین (orthographic variant of این). چون بود روزگار is a rhetorical question 'how/what sort of was the time/fate', with چون = 'how' and بود = past 'was'.
segments.s250-g70: سر آرد here uses the noun سر + verb آرد ('brings/causes to occur') in an idiomatic sense 'bring about/ happen'. مگر is used rhetorically ('what else / could it be that'), and بر من این کردگار is the prepositional target 'upon me, this Creator' (کردگار = the Maker/God). The whole clause asks what further misfortune the Creator could bring upon the speaker.
segments.s250-g71: چو here functions temporally/causally ('as' / 'when'). The epithet بیچاره modifies the name بیژن ('poor, unfortunate Bijan'). ب + دان is written as separated elements in the segmentation but functions as the demonstrative/locative 'into/that' (here 'in that'); since دان in the provided senses does not match this usage, it is marked -1. ژرف چاه is a compound 'deep pit/well' where the adjective precedes the noun in Persian order is reversed poetically.
segments.s250-g72: نبیند is a negative present/optative form of دیدن meaning 'does not see'. The sequence شب و روز خورشید و ماه lists the visible cycles and luminaries ('night and day, sun and moon') as the objects he no longer sees; word order places the verb before its coordinated objects for poetic effect.
segments.s250-g49: 'ندانم' is the negative present of 'دانستن' (I do not know). 'همی' here is an emphatic/poetic particle (roughly 'at all' / 'indeed') placed after the verb for emphasis. The sequence 'گیو و گودرز را' is the definite direct object marked by 'را' (object marker) and lists two proper names joined by 'و' (and).
segments.s250-g50: 'نه' negates the following verb phrase, and 'پیموده‌ام' is the perfect (past) first-person singular of 'پیمودن' meaning 'I have traversed/crossed'. 'هرگز' is an adverb strengthening the negation ('never'). 'آن مرز را' is the definite object ('that border') with 'را' marking it; word order is verb before object in this clause.
segments.s250-g51: 'بفرمود' is a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he ordered/said'. 'تا' introduces the subordinate clause meaning 'that' / 'so that' here. 'خوردنی هرچ بود' is literally 'whatever was edible/whatever food there was' with 'خوردنی' functioning as 'food/edible thing' and 'هرچ' = 'whatever'; 'بود' is the past copula ('there was').
segments.s250-g52: 'نهادند' is the past plural of نهادن ('they placed/set'). The prepositional phrase 'در پیش' ('in front of/before') is followed by 'درویش' (the dervish, here the beneficiary/recipient). 'زود' is an adverb meaning 'quickly/soon' modifying the placing action.
segments.s250-g53: 'یکایک' is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'one after another'. 'سخن کرد' is a common compound where 'کرد' (did) combines with 'سخن' to mean 'he spoke'. 'ازو' ('from/of him') marks the person to whom the request/asking refers. 'خواستار' here functions as 'one who asks' or 'petitioner', describing the speech-act.
segments.s250-g54: 'که' introduces the content clause ('that/why ...'), leading into the reported question. 'با تو' means 'toward you' or 'about you'. 'چرا' is 'why'. 'شد' is the past tense of 'شدن' ('became'). 'دژم روزگار' is a noun phrase meaning 'grim/sullen time/fate' (i.e. fate turned harsh); word order places the predicate 'شد' before the adjective phrase describing the state.
segments.s250-g55: 'چه پرسی' is a second-person present interrogative phrase meaning 'why do you ask' or 'what do you ask'. 'ز' is the poetic/older form of 'از' meaning 'about/regarding' or 'from'. The list 'گردان و شاه و سپاه' enumerates 'troops/detachments, the king, and the army' as the topics of the question.
segments.s250-g56: 'چه داری' is 'what do you have' or idiomatically 'what business/concern do you have'. 'همی' here means 'still' (continuative/emphatic), so 'چه داری همی' = 'what do you still have'. 'راه ایران' is 'the road/way of Iran' and 'نگاه' (noun) here means 'watching/keeping guard' or 'attendance' (literally 'gaze/look'), so the clause asks why the addressee continues to concern themselves with guarding/looking after Iran's roads.
segments.s250-g225: سوی + noun phrase marks the direction toward which the action is done; کردگار جهان is a nominal phrase 'Creator (of the) world'. کرد is the past-tense verb here (from کردن) and together with سر (literally 'head') forms the verb phrase سر کردن 'to turn/bow the head' — the object/directional phrase precedes the verb in this poetic ordering.
segments.s250-g226: که‌ای is an archaic/vocative-like address used to call or invoke (here introducing a direct address to God); پاک و بخشنده و دادگر are coordinate adjectives stacked as a vocative description ('O Pure, Generous, and Just'). There is no copula; the adjectives directly qualify the addressee.
segments.s250-g227: ز (poetic) = 'from' or 'against' introduces the source/thing to be guarded against; هر بد = 'every bad/evil'. تو باشی is a hortatory/optative use of باشی ('may you be' / 'be you'), addressing the addressee and expressing a wish; مرا (here split in the edition) is the object 'me' and را is the object marker; دستگیر is used as a predicative noun/adjective meaning 'helper, succourer' — so the clause means 'May you be my helper against every evil.'
segments.s250-g228: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; زن is the imperative of زدن ('strike' / 'smite') here, not the noun 'woman'. بر + noun marks the target ('upon/against'); دل و جان is a coordinated object 'heart and life' (دل = heart, جان = life/soul); بدخواه modifies تیر (an enemy/ill-wisher's arrow). Word order is poetic but the sense is 'You strike (away) the ill-wisher's arrow aimed at my heart and life.'
segments.s250-g229: بده is the 2nd-person imperative 'give!'. داد here means 'justice' (a noun) and stands as the thing requested; من marks the affected person ('my' / 'me') — the order is poetic ('give justice to me'). زآنک is a causal conjunction 'because/for that', and بیداد کرد is a past verbal phrase 'committed injustice' (کرد = 'did/committed'), explaining the reason for the request.
segments.s250-g230: تو دانی = 'you know' (present). غمان من is a possessive construction where the noun (غمان, 'sorrows') is followed by the pronoun من to mean 'my sorrows' (classical/poetic order). و داغ و درد adds two more items in the list: داغ 'burning grief/wound' and درد 'pain/affliction', completing the catalogue of the speaker's sufferings.
segments.s250-g231: مگر introduces an exception/condition here meaning 'unless'. بازیابم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I recover / may I recover' (used as the wished-for condition). بر و بوم is the fixed pair 'land and abode' = 'homeland', and را is the definite object marker attached to that object.
segments.s250-g232: نمانم is first-person negative subjunctive/optative 'may I not remain' or simply 'I will not remain' in the sense of a consequence; بننگ ('به ننگ' contracted) means 'in shame / to disgrace' and functions adverbially/predicatively; اختر شوم combines the noun اختر 'star' (used metaphorically) with شوم, the 1st-person present/subjunctive of شدن 'to become' — together indicating 'I shall become a (disgraced) star' (a poetic way to say 'I shall be disgraced'). The final را is an emphatic/poetic enclitic.
segments.s250-g9: بکام تو بادا is an optative wish: بکام (ب-کام) 'to one's desire' + تو 'you' yields 'to your desire'; بادا is the classical optative particle 'may it be'. سپهر بلند is a noun phrase 'lofty/heavenly sky' with adjective بلند after the noun (typical poetic word order). The whole segment reads 'May the high heavens be to your liking.'
segments.s250-g10: ز introduces an ablative/agentive 'from'; چشم here functions as 'gaze/eye' (the harmful glance). بدانت is an archaic/poetic form attached to چشم meaning roughly 'evil one/hostile (toward you)' (lexical ambiguity — see senses). مبادا is another optative/negative particle 'may it not be' and گزند is the noun 'harm'. The clause literally: 'From the evil eye of (toward) you may there not be harm.'
segments.s250-g11: هر امید دل را is a definite object phrase: هر 'every', امید 'hope', دل را 'of the heart' with را marking the object. که بستی is a relative clause introduced by که 'that' with بستی (past, 2nd sing.) 'you tied/placed/attached'. میان here is adverbial 'in the midst/within'. Together: 'every hope of the heart that you placed within'.
segments.s250-g12: ز marks source/ablative 'from'; رنجی is an indefinite/partitive form of رنج 'suffering/hardship'. که بردی is a clause 'which you bore' (بردی past 2nd sing., 'you carried/underwent'). مبادت is the optative negative 'may it not be' (classical variant of مباد), and زیان 'loss/detriment' is the outcome being wished away. Literal reading: 'May there be no loss from the suffering you have borne.'
segments.s250-g13: همیشه 'always' opens the wish. خرد بادت آموزگار is another optative clause: خرد 'wisdom' + بادت (باد + enclitic ت) is the optative 'may it be to you', and آموزگار 'teacher' follows as predicate/noun. Word order places the wished-for noun (خرد) first, then the optative verb, then the role (آموزگار).
segments.s250-g14: This is a coordinated nominal phrase describing the wished conditions: خنک بوم ایران 'a cool/pleasant homeland of Iran' (adjective خنک before noun بوم, followed by the genitive/identifier ایران) and و خوش روزگار 'and pleasant/happy times' (خوش modifying روزگار). There is no finite verb here; the clause continues the earlier optative sense.
segments.s250-g15: چه آگاهی استت is an interrogative phrase: چه 'what', آگاهی 'knowledge/information'. استت is an archaic/copular form meaning roughly 'is (to you)' or 'do you have' in classical Persian, so the whole unit reads 'what information do you have'. ز گردان شاه is an ablative/genitive phrase 'about/from the king's troop', linking the question to its topic.
segments.s250-g16: A list of sources introduced by repeated ز 'from/about': ز گیو و ز گودرز و ایران سپاه names Giv and Goudarz and then 'the army of Iran' (note the inversion ایران سپاه = سپاهِ ایران). The repeated ز stresses 'from/about X and from/about Y and from/about the Iranian host.'
segments.s250-g17: نیامد is the negative past/present-perfect-type verb 'did not come' placed at the start (typical in classical Persian for emphasis). The prepositional phrase به ایران ('to/into Iran') follows, then ز بیژن خبر ('news of Bijan') — ز marks the source/aboutness ('about/from Bijan') and خبر is the noun 'news' that is the thing that did not come.
segments.s250-g18: نیایش is a noun 'prayer/supplication' serving as the grammatical subject. نخواهد is the negative auxiliary/future 'will not' (here functioning like 'will not be / will not prove'). بدن is 'the body' and چاره‌گر is a nominal predicate/adjective 'healer/remedial' — together: 'prayer will not be the body's healer.' This is a compact, verbless-predicative construction typical of poetic Persian where the auxiliary links subject and predicate.
segments.s250-g19: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause ('for/that, when'); چون here means 'as'/'when' or 'such as', and او is the pronominal subject 'he'. جوانی is an appositive noun 'a youth/a young man' describing او. ز + گودرزیان means 'from/among the Gudarzis' (classical poetic genitive/source), so the whole chunk: 'when he, a youth of the Gudarzis...'.
segments.s250-g20: همی is the classical continuous/progressive particle ('continually/was wont to') attached to the verb form بگسلاند ('would break/tear apart'); together they give an iterative or habitual sense. بسختی is an adverb modifying the verb ('with severity/force' or 'with difficulty'), and میان is a locative adverb 'in the midst/between'. Word order places the adverbial modifier after the verb, as is common in older Persian.
segments.s250-g21: بسودست here is an archaic adverbial phrase meaning roughly 'on the right side' (literally 'to the advantageous/right hand side') placed before the possessed noun پایش ('his foot'). ز + بند گران ('from/by heavy fetters') indicates the instrument/cause — i.e. his foot was held by heavy fetters. The line is verbless/nominal, which is common in epic poetry: the state is described without an explicit copula.
segments.s250-g22: دو دستش = 'his two hands' (numeral + noun + possessive suffix ش). ز introduces the instrument/means 'by/with', and مسمار آهنگران is a genitive phrase 'the nails/pegs of the blacksmiths' (i.e. iron nails/pegs made by smiths). Again the clause is nominal (the verb 'were bound' is implied by context).
segments.s250-g23: کشیده is the past participle of کشیدن ('to draw/drag') used as a resultative adjective meaning 'having been dragged' or simply 'dragged'. بزنجیر is be- + زنجیر, a classical prepositional/qualifying phrase meaning 'in chains' or 'with chains' and attaches to کشیده. و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. بسته is the past participle of بستن ('to bind/fasten'), 'bound' and here works as another resultative/participial description. ببند is an archaic/stylistic verbal form related to بندن/بستن; in Ferdowsi it functions here as a resultive/actional verb closely tied to the participle بسته — not as a contemporary direct imperative — conveying 'bound/fastened' as part of the past narrative. Together the group describes the subject as dragged in chains and secured tightly.
segments.s250-g24: همه modifies چاه and means 'the whole/entire' (i.e. the entire well). چاه is 'well' (a water-well). پرخون is an adjective 'full of blood'/'bloodied' describing the state of the well. آن مستمند is a noun phrase 'that poor/needful man' specifying whose blood it is; classical Persian often omits a preposition like از, so پرخونِ آن مستمند is understood as 'with the blood of that poor man.' The whole segment therefore states that the entire well was (or became) filled with that poor man's blood.
segments.s250-g249: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از + چشم خورشید is a genitive/ablative phrase 'from the eye of the sun' (i.e., the sun's sight); شد ناپدید is a compound predication: شد (past of شدن, 'became') + ناپدید (predicate 'vanished/hidden'), so literally 'became vanished from the sun's eye' — poetically 'when the sun disappeared'. Word order places the verb after the noun phrase, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s250-g250: شب تیره is a noun + adjective 'the dark night'; بر کوه دامن کشید is a verbal phrase where دامن (hem/skirt) is the object or image and کشید is the past tense of 'کشیدن' used here as 'to draw/pull' (poetic: 'drew its skirt over the mountain'). The preposition بر ('over/on') marks the target (کوه).
segments.s250-g251: بدانگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment/thereupon'. که introduces the subordinate clause. آرام گیرد is a verbal phrase where آرام (calm) is the predicate and گیرد (from گرفتن) is used in the sense 'become seized by / take on' — here 'becomes calm'; جهان is the subject placed after the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s250-g252: شود is 'becomes' (from شدن). آشکارای is a poetic/adverbial form of آشکارا 'made manifest/evidently'; گیتی نهان is a noun phrase 'the hidden world' (گیتی = world, نهان = hidden). Together the clause means 'the hidden world becomes revealed.' Note the adjective نهان follows the noun, a normal Persian order.
segments.s250-g253: که is the subordinating/continuative 'that/so'. لشکر کشد is a poetic present/habitual or narrative use of کشیدن meaning 'to muster/marshal an army' (here 'draws an army'); تیره شب is the agent 'the dark night' and پیش روز means 'before the day' (پیش = before). Word order is inverted for poetical effect (verb before its subject).
segments.s250-g254: بگردد is from گشتن with the sense 'turns' or 'appears/comes round' (narrative present). سر هور is a genitive-like phrase 'the head/crest of Hoor (splendor/light)', where هور is an epithet for brilliant light; گیتی فروز is a compound epithet 'world-illuminating' (گیتی = world, فروز = bright/illuminating). The clause describes the turning/appearance of that shining crest which lights the world.
segments.s250-g255: منیژه is the proper name subject. سبک آتشی is a noun phrase where سبک ('light/slight') qualifies آتشی ('fiery, a fire/flame') — here 'a slight/light flame' or 'a small spark'. برفروخت is a poetic past of برفروختن meaning 'kindled' or 'set alight'.
segments.s250-g256: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/so that'. چشم شب قیرگون is a genitive phrase 'the night’s pitch-black eye' (چشم = eye, شب = night, قیرگون = pitch-black). را is the definite/direct object marker, and بسوخت is the simple past 'burned' (transitive) — 'it burned the black eye of night.'
segments.s250-g185: منیژه is the proper name Manijeh (subject). بیامد is the simple past 'came'. بکردار is an adjective 'of good/virtuous conduct' modifying the subject. باد here is the optative particle (a wish), so the phrase literally reads 'Manijeh came — may (she) be virtuous': adjective + باد expresses a blessing/wish about the subject.
segments.s250-g186: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. برستم should be read as بِ رستم = 'to Rostam' (the preposition بِ/به fused with the name رستم), marking the indirect object/destination. پیامش is پیام + enclitic -ش 'his' (referring to Bijan) = 'his message'. بداد is an archaic/perfect-form of دادن 'gave' (the prosthetic b- appears in classical Persian verbs); together 'پیامش بداد' = 'she gave his message'.
segments.s250-g187: چو + بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو here = when). گفتار آن خوب روی = 'the speech/words of that fair-faced one': گفتار 'speech/words', آن 'that/the', خوب‌روی (good/beautiful-faced) is a compound descriptive phrase where خوب modifies روی ('face' = fair-faced). Word order is typical: verb (بشنید) followed by the object phrase.
segments.s250-g188: کزان = 'from which' (relative/locative sense) introducing source; راه دور آمده = 'who had come a distant way' (راه = road/path, دور = distant, آمده = come/arrived). The doubled پوی پوی is a poetic reduplication meaning 'swift, hurrying' or 'with haste' (intensifying the adverbial idea). The whole segment qualifies the person whose speech was heard.
segments.s250-g189: بدانست رستم = 'Rostam knew/perceived it' (بدانست is past 'knew/realized', رستم is the subject in apposition). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; بیژن سخن = 'Bijan (his) speech' or 'Bijan had spoken' where سخن is the noun 'speech/words' forming the object/content of that clause.
segments.s250-g190: گشادست here is an archaic/poetic verbal/adjectival form meaning 'opened/began' (سخن گشاد = 'began to speak'); it states that the speech was opened/uttered. بر = 'upon' or 'on'; لاله and سروبن are floral/metaphorical epithets (لاله = tulip/flower, سروبن = cypress‑like) used poetically to describe the speaker's beauty or countenance. The segment therefore pictures the act of speaking together with ornamental metaphors.
segments.s250-g191: ببخشود is the past tense 'he forgave' (or 'he granted [pardon]'); و is the coordinating 'and'; گفتش = 'he said to her' (گفت + enclitic ش = to her). که‌ای خوب‌چهر is a vocative: که‌ای here introduces direct speech 'O ...', and خوب‌چهر is 'fair‑faced' (a form of address). The clause precedes the quoted or reported wish.
segments.s250-g192: This is a short optative/wish clause introduced by که. یزدان = 'God' (subject of the wish). ترا = تو + object marker (ت second‑person clitic + را as object/topic marker) = 'you'. ز + و + مبراد is an archaic cluster meaning roughly 'from him may (it) not be taken away' (ز = from); مبراد is an old subjunctive/negative‑sense verb form here ('may not remove/deprive') and thus expresses 'may God not deprive you of ...'. مهر = 'love/affection' is the object of that wish: 'may God not remove love from you because of him.' Several items (the exact archaic morphology of مبراد and the small clitic و) are irregular/poetic; the net sense is a blessing that God not take away your love on his account.
segments.s250-g73: A nominal list joined by و (and). بغل is a noun meaning 'embrace' (here an instrument/way), بمسمار is a compound meaning 'with a nail' / 'nailed', and بند گران is a noun (بند, 'fetter, bond') modified by the adjective گران 'heavy'. The whole phrase names the means/instruments before the verb.
segments.s250-g74: همی is an emphatic/continuative particle prefixed to the verb phrase; مرگ is the subject 'death'; خواهد is the auxiliary expressing future/inevitability ('will'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; یزدان 'God' is the source; بران is an adverbial/pronominal locution 'upon that / upon them' linking the source to the action. Together: 'death will come from God upon them.'
segments.s250-g75: مرا (م + را here) functions as the accusative 'me' placed before the verb for emphasis; درد بر درد is a reduplicative idiom 'pain upon pain' with بر marking 'upon'; بفزود is a present/imperfective form (prefixed ب‑) meaning 'adds / causes to increase'; زین is an archaic demonstrative 'this' (lit. 'by/through this') referring back to whatever was just mentioned and linking to the verb.
segments.s250-g76: نم دیدگانم = 'the moisture/tears of my eyes', where دیدگانم is 'my eyes' with the possessive suffix; بپالود (prefixed ب‑) is a verb meaning 'purified/cleansed' in imperfective; the final زین again is the demonstrative 'by/through this', linking the purification to the same cause mentioned previously.
segments.s250-g77: کنون is the adverb 'now'; گرت is an enclitic conditional form (گر + ت) 'if you / if it be that'; باشد is the subjunctive/optative 'may be / there be' used to frame the hypothetical; بایران describes the place 'to the waste/desert' and modifies گذر 'passage/crossing'. Word order places the time/adverb and conditional marker before the hypothetical predicate.
segments.s250-g78: ز is the preposition 'from'; گودرز and کشواد are proper names (Goudarz, Keshvad) in sequence; یابی is a subjunctive/optative form ('you may find' / 'you will obtain'); خبر is the object 'news'. The verb یابی takes خبر as its object and the names mark the source of the news.
segments.s250-g79: بدرگاه 'in the presence of / at the court of' takes خسرو (the king) as its complement; مگر is a modal/introductory particle here used in an archaic sense ('perhaps / maybe') introducing the possibility; گیو را is the object (Giv) marked with را for definiteness/emphasis. The phrase establishes a possible sighting at court.
segments.s250-g80: ببینی is a present/subjunctive form 'you may see / you might see'; و‌گر is the conditional 'and if / but if' introducing an alternative; رستم is the proper name 'Rostam'; نیو را appears as an alternate object phrase (likely a name or epithet) with را marking it. The clause offers an alternative: if not Giv, then Rostam Niu.
segments.s250-g25: نیابم is a negative first‑person verb form meaning 'I cannot / I do not find' (classical Persian form with ن-). The causal preposition ز introduces the reason: ز درویشی خویش = 'because of his/one's (or my) dervish-ness'; خویش is a possessive form built from خوی + ش and here marks the 'own/one's' relation. خواب (sleep) is the direct object placed after the cause phrase—literal order 'I‑find‑not from dervishhood‑own sleep' → 'I find no sleep because of my/one's dervish state.'
segments.s250-g26: ز + verbal noun نالیدن 'from/because of wailing' marks the cause: 'because of his wailing' (او = 'he'). دو چشمم is the noun 'two eyes' plus the enclitic -م marking possession ('my two eyes'). پر آب is a postpositive adjective phrase 'full of water' (آب here = tears), so the clause reads 'because of his lamenting my two eyes are full of water.'
segments.s250-g27: بترسید is a past/perfect verb meaning 'was frightened / feared' (verb precedes the subject here for poetic emphasis). رستم is the subject. ز گفتار اوی is a prepositional phrase 'because of / from his speech' giving the cause of the fear (اوی = 'his' / 'he').
segments.s250-g28: یکی here functions adverbially as 'one / a single' modifying بانگ 'a cry, a shout'. برزد is a past action 'struck/let out' (here 'uttered/raised' a cry). براندش is the past verb 'drove him away', followed by ز روی 'from before him / from his face' indicating removal from Rostam's presence.
segments.s250-g29: بدو is a prepositional/dative clitic 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is the verb 'said'. کز introduces the reported imperative/clause ('that' / 'that from'), and پیش من دور شو is the command 'be far / go away from before me'—پیش 'before/in the presence of', من 'me', دور 'away', شو the imperative 'become/go'.
segments.s250-g30: نه ... نه ... is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor ...'. شناسم here is the verb 'I know / I recognize' (present/persistent sense). خسرو and سالارنو are the two items negated: 'the king' (خسرو) and 'the commander / Salarno' (سالارنو) — together: 'I know neither the king nor the commander.'
segments.s250-g31: ندارم is the present negative 'I do not have / I have not'. ز before proper names here means 'concerning / about' (classical/poetic use): 'about Goudarz and Giv' (گودرز و گیو). آگهی is the noun 'information / news' and is the object of ندارم, yielding 'I have no information about Goudarz and Giv.'
segments.s250-g32: که introduces the explanatory/result clause 'that / for'. مغزم is 'my brain/mind' (مغز + enclitic -م = my). ز گفتار کردی is 'you (کردی) by your speech (ز گفتار)'; کردی is second‑person past 'you did / you made'. تهی is the predicate adjective 'empty'. Together: 'that you by your words have made my mind empty.'
segments.s250-g217: مرا گفت = 'he said to me'; مرا is the object pronoun 'me' marked by را, followed by گفت 'said' (past). چُنانِ subordinate clause follows with چون 'when' and then the predicate 'تیره گردد هوا' where the verb گرد(د) 'become' appears before the subject هوا 'the air/sky' — a common classical Persian order (verb then subject). گردد here is a present/subjunctive-type form used in subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s250-g218: شب از چنگ خورشید یابد رها = 'night finds release from the sun's grip.' از introduces the source 'from the grip of the sun' (چنگ خورشید). یابد is a present/subjunctive or simple present 'finds/attains' and رها here functions as 'free/unbound' (result/state). Word order places the verb before the predicative adjective 'رها'.
segments.s250-g219: بکردار کوه آتشی برفروز = a compact noun phrase: بکردار 'virtuous' qualifies کوه 'mountain' (i.e. 'the virtuous mountain'); آتشی is a deverbal/adjectival form 'fiery' or 'a fire' with the izâfa-like -ی, and برفروز postpositively modifies it 'bright/illuminating'. Together they describe lighting or presence of a bright fire on that mountain.
segments.s250-g220: که introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that'; سنگ و سر چاه is a coordinated noun phrase 'the stone(s) and the top/mouth of the well' (سر here = top/mouth). گردد 'become' is the verb, and چو روز 'like day' is the simile complement. Word order places the verb before the simile 'چو روز'.
segments.s250-g221: بدان is a locative/adverbial 'to that (place)' formed from ب- + دان in classical usage; تا introduces a purpose clause 'so that' and governs the subjunctive ببینم 'I may see'. سر چاه را is the definite object (را marks it), 'the mouth/top of the well.' The verb ببینم is first-person singular subjunctive expressing purpose.
segments.s250-g222: بدان روشنی = 'with that light' (instrumental/ablative sense of بدان). بسپرم is first-person singular present/subjunctive 'I will entrust/commit' or 'I will set' and takes راه را 'the way/the road' as its definite object (را). The construction expresses intention: by that light I will secure/guide the road.
segments.s250-g223: بفرمود بیژن = 'he commanded/told Bijan' (befarmud is a polite/past-reporting verb). The clause که آتش فروز is an indirect imperative/command introduced by که: آتش 'fire' is the object and فروز is a present/imperative form 'ignite/illuminate' addressed to Bijan implicitly.
segments.s250-g224: که again introduces result/purpose 'so that'; رستیم is a past/result form meaning 'we were saved / we escape' (here purpose/result: 'that we two may be delivered'). هر دو 'both (of us)' modifies the verb, ز is the classical preposition 'from', and تاریک روز 'dark day' = 'darkness of the day/night-time gloom' is the source from which they are delivered.
segments.s250-g153: منیژه is the subject (proper name). خروشید is a past, intransitive verb meaning 'cried out/roared' (3sg); و simply links verbs; نالید is another past intransitive verb 'moaned/wailed'. زار here modifies نالید (adverbial/qualifying), meaning 'piteously' or 'lamentingly'. The verbs are in simple past and coordinate: 'she cried and moaned.'
segments.s250-g154: که introduces an exclamatory/dependent clause here ('that' / 'how'); بر من آمد is a Persian idiom 'came upon me' = 'befell me' (آمد is used in the sense 'happen'). چه is an exclamatory interrogative 'what/how' (what has happened), بد روزگار is a noun phrase 'evil/unfavorable fate' (بد qualifying روزگار). Word order is idiomatic SOV-like within the idiom: 'what (چه) came (آمد) upon me (بر من) — bad fate.'
segments.s250-g155: دریغ is an interjection 'alas/pity'. آن شده is a verblike phrase 'has become that' (شده = become, here pointing back to her misfortune). روزگاران من = 'my times/fortunes' — Persian puts the possessor after the noun, so من is possessive here ('my'). The whole clause laments that her fortunes have come to a sorry state.
segments.s250-g156: دل‌خسته is a compound adjective 'heart-sore / weary of heart'. و simply coordinates. چشم باران is a poetic compound 'rain-eyed' i.e. eyes like rain — figuratively 'eyes full of tears' (باران used figuratively). من again follows the noun as possessive ('my').
segments.s250-g157: بدادم is a past-tense verbal form here meaning 'I gave / I surrendered' (an older/poetic form of دادم with a prefixed b-). ببیژن is the old prefixed dative 'to Bijan' (ب + Bijan = 'to Bijan'). تن و خان و مان are a triad of nouns 'body (تن), house/home (خان), and household/remaining possessions (مان)' listed in a coordinate series as direct objects of دادم.
segments.s250-g158: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. گشت is the past form of گشتن 'became / turned (into)', used here as 'has become'. بر من is the dative-like phrase 'upon me' (i.e. 'for me' / 'to me'), and چنین بدگمان is a predicative phrase 'so suspicious' / 'filled with such suspicion'. Together: 'now such suspicion has come upon me.'
segments.s250-g159: همان is a demonstrative 'that / the very (same)'. گنج دینار = 'treasure (of) dinars' (a genitive/attributive compound: treasure made up of dinars). و links the two items; تاج گهر = 'the crown of jewels' (gهر qualifies تاج). This is a noun list fronted as the object of the following verb.
segments.s250-g160: بتاراج is a fused prepositional/adverbial phrase (به تاراج) meaning 'to plunder / to pillage' — here it marks the fate of the named goods. دادم is the verb 'I gave' (past). همه سربسر is an emphatic adverbial phrase 'all, entirely / utterly' modifying the verb: 'I gave (them) all away to plunder.' The object is placed before the verb, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s250-g161: پدر گشته بیزار = a short clause with a subject (پدر) + perfective/resultative participle (گشته) + predicate adjective (بیزار). گشته is the classical/perfect participle of شدن used here as 'has become/has grown', and بیزار is a post‑verbal adjective meaning 'averse/estranged'. The conjunction و links this clause to the following noun phrase.
segments.s250-g162: برهنه دوان بر سر انجمن = adjective (برهنه 'naked') precedes the verb (دوان 'run, rush'), a common poetic order; بر سر انجمن is a prepositional phrase ('on/upon the head/top of the assembly' → 'around/in the assembly'). Together it describes the relatives' action: they run naked about the assembly.
segments.s250-g163: ز امید بیژن شدم ناامید = the prepositional/causal phrase ز امید بیژن ('from/about the hope for Bijan' or 'regarding Bijan's hope') introduces the reason; شدم is the past of شدن 'I became' and ناامید is the resulting adjective 'hopeless'. The sequence expresses loss of hope concerning Bijan.
segments.s250-g164: جهانم سیاه و دو دیده سپید = noun + possessive suffix (جهانم 'my world') followed by predicate adjective سیاه 'dark/gloomy'. The conjunction و links the next clause, where دوه دیده ('two eyes') is the noun phrase and سپید ('white') is the predicate adjective; the copula is omitted as typical in Persian.
segments.s250-g165: بپوشد همی راز بر من چنین = بپوشد is a present/subjunctive/resultive form of پوشیدن here meaning 'is/shall conceal' (the prefixed ب- is the classical present/stative marker); همی is an archaic continuous/emphatic particle marking ongoing or general action. راز is the object and بر من ('upon me' / 'from me') shows whose knowledge is affected; چنین 'thus' is an adverb qualifying the manner.
segments.s250-g166: تو داناتری‌ای جهان‌آفرین = direct address. تو is the second‑person pronoun, داناتری‌ای is a comparative/adjectival form ('more knowing' + vocative clitic ای), and جهان‌آفرین is a vocative apposition 'O world‑creator' (creator). Word order places the pronoun before the predicate adjective for emphasis.
segments.s250-g167: بدو گفت بیژن همه راستست = بدو is the enclitic/old form meaning 'to him/to it' (به + او), گفت is the past verb 'said' (here rendered with a noun sense in the provided list but functioning as 'he said'), and بیژن is the speaker/agent. همه راستست is a short predication: همه 'all' + راستست 'it is true' (راست + است fused), i.e. 'all that is said is true.'
segments.s250-g168: ز من کار تو جمله برکاستست = a causal/agentive phrase: ز من 'because of me' or 'on my account'; کار تو 'your work' (possessive sequence), جمله 'all/entire', and برکاستست is an archaic/resultative verbal form meaning 'has been reduced/undone/has come to nothing' with the final -ست corresponding to 'is/has'. The clause states that, owing to the speaker, the addressee's whole undertaking has been undone.
segments.s250-g201: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from / out of'. The phrase arranges noun phrases in old/poetic order: 'بیشه' (grove) followed by an attributive 'فرازآر' (lit. 'uplifting' or 'lofty') modifying 'هیزم' (firewood), and 'بروز' (appearance) names the event — literally, 'from the grove a lofty firewood (pile) came into appearance'.
segments.s250-g202: Night is marked first (شب) and the verb آید (comes) follows it; یکی specifies 'one/a single' and modifies آتشی ('a fire/torch') which is described by برفروز ('illuminating' used adjectivally). The line uses the simple present/past-narrative feel typical of epic poetry (here rendered as a past event in translation).
segments.s250-g203: This is a straightforward nominative + cause + copula: 'منیژه' is the subject, 'ز گفتار او' is a prepositional phrase 'because / from his speech', and 'شاد شد' is the past (perfective) of شدن meaning 'became happy'. In narrative Persian the auxiliary/past verb 'شد' finalizes the predication.
segments.s250-g204: دلش is 'her heart' with the enclitic pronoun ش (= her). ز again marks cause ('from / of') and links to the following nominal; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying آزاد شد ('was freed' — passive/resultative reading of شدن). The segment as a whole says her heart was wholly freed from (its) sorrows.
segments.s250-g205: بیامد دوان is a compound narrative phrase: بیامد 'came' with the adverbial/participle دوان 'running/rushed', so 'he came running'. تا here means 'as far as / until' and the sequence 'ب' + 'دان' together form the deictic 'بدان' = 'to that'. چاهسار is the place 'well‑field / place of wells' and is the goal reached.
segments.s250-g206: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'چاهسار'. بودش is the past copula with enclitic (literally 'it was to him/there was'), بچاه = 'in/into the well', اندرون = 'within/inside', and غمگسار is a noun meaning a fellow sufferer / companion in sorrow. Together: 'which had inside the well a companion in sorrow.'
segments.s250-g207: بگفتش is the past-tense reporting verb 'said to him/her' (بـ- prefix + گفت + enclitic), که introduces the reported content, and دادم is the first‑person past 'I gave/delivered' with سراسر ('entire/throughout') modifying پیام ('message/tidings'). The clause is a reported-speech construction.
segments.s250-g208: بدان here is the deictic 'to that' (again b + دان fused) pointing to 'مرد' (man/hero). فرخ is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' and نیک‌نام 'well‑named / well‑reputed' modifies the man; پی is part of the adjectival phrase (common in classical Persian epithets) and functions to link the epithets — together identifying the recipient of the message.
segments.s250-g209: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed word order (adverb + past verb + noun) meaning “gave such an answer” (چنین = thus, داد = gave, پاسخ = answer). که introduces the reported content clause. آنم درست is an archaic, concise copular formula: آنم (= آن + enclitic, used here as ‘that is so/it is so’) and درست = ‘correct/true’, so together = “that is true.”
segments.s250-g210: که again introduces the subordinate clause ‘that…’. بیژن is the subject (the hero’s name). بنام here means ‘by name / by the name (of)’, و = and, نشانم is نشان + enclitic -م functioning as a possessive/object marker ‘my/for me’ (i.e. ‘sign/token relating to me’), and بجست is the past tense of جَستن ‘to seek’ (here ‘sought’). The clause therefore says that Bijan sought (for) him by name and by sign.
segments.s250-g211: تو is the (stressed) second-person subject ‘you’. با + داغ دل = a prepositional phrase ‘with the burning/brand of the heart’ (داغ دل = deep grief, wound of the heart). چند = interrogative ‘how long / how much’. پویی is the present/continuous second‑person form of پوییدن ‘to go / to roam’ (classical spelling), so تو ... چند پویی همی = ‘how long will you keep going’. همی is an archaic continuous/progressive marker (‘still/continually’).
segments.s250-g212: که here introduces the consequence or result clause ‘so that…’. رخ را is the direct object marked by را (‘the face’). بخوناب is a prefixed adjective/adverbial meaning ‘in blood / blood-soaked’ (ب‑ + خوناب), شویی is the present subjunctive/indicative of شستن ‘to wash’ (here passive/experiential: ‘be washed / be bathed’), and the final همی repeats the continuous sense. Together: ‘so that your face will be continually bathed in blood.’
segments.s250-g213: کنون = now / at present. چون here has the causal sense ‘since / as’, so چون درست آمد = ‘since/now that a true (درست) thing has come’. آمد is the past of آمدن ‘came/has come’. از تو نشان = ‘a sign/token from/of you’ (از + تو + نشان). The clause sets the temporal/casual background: ‘Now that a true sign of you has appeared…’.
segments.s250-g214: ببینی is the present/subjunctive form of دیدن with the b‑ prefix used in classical Persian to indicate an action in the present/near-future (‘you will see / you shall see’). سر is the direct object ‘head(s)’, and the following nouns form a compact attributive/genitive chain: تیغ = ‘sword’, مردم = ‘people/men’, and کشان is the adjectival/participial suffix ‘‑keshān / ‑keshan’ meaning ‘dragging/ killing’ (i.e. ‘men‑slaying’). The noun cluster thus pictures heads in relation to swords that slay men: ‘you will see heads by the men‑slaying sword(s).’
segments.s250-g215: زمین را is the definite object ‘the earth’ (را marks the object). بدرانم is a first‑person verbal form meaning ‘I will rend/tear apart’ (classical/poetic formation), so زمین را بدرانم = ‘I will tear up the earth’. اکنون = now. بچنگ is an adverbial phrase literally ‘into (my) grasp’ (ب + چنگ), indicating taking hold or seizing; syntactically it links to the following clause describing what the speaker will do next.
segments.s250-g216: بپروین = toward Parvin (Parvin here a proper name/constellation) or ‘to Parvin’ (b‑ prefix + proper noun indicating direction). براندازم is first‑person singular present/future ‘I will overturn / I will throw down’ (براندازیدن = to overthrow). آسوده سنگ is a noun phrase ‘the resting/at‑peace stone’ (آسوده = at peace/settled; سنگ = stone/rock), and placed after the verb it is the object of براندازم: ‘I will overturn the settled stone.’ The sequence portrays an enormous, violent action (tearing earth, seizing, hurling/overturning a great rock).
segments.s250-g137: زمانی is a temporal adverb placed first and here means 'for a while'. فرو ماند is a past-tense 3rd-person singular verb phrase: فرو (an adverbial/particle indicating downward/deep) + ماند (remained/was left) — together implying 'sank down / was overcome'. زان کار سخت is a prepositional/agent phrase with زان (poetic = از آن, 'from/by that') + کار سخت 'that hard deed', giving the cause of his falling.
segments.s250-g138: بگفت is the past-tense 3rd-person 'he said' (subject omitted because it refers back to the previous actor). The following clause is reported speech/問い: این 'this' + چه as an interrogative/exclamative 'what/how' + خندست is an archaic/copular form (خنده + است) meaning 'it is laughter' or 'it is laughable'. ای نیکبخت is a vocative phrase 'O fortunate one' addressing the addressee.
segments.s250-g139: شگفت آمدش is a compact phrase meaning 'a wonder/marvel came to him' — آمد with the clitic -ش 'to him'. داستانی بزد uses بزد (past 3rd-person of زدن) with داستانی as its object; in classical poetic usage 'داستان زدن' = 'to begin/tell a story', so بزد here means 'he launched into/told' the tale.
segments.s250-g140: که introduces the content/clause of the tale ('that...'). دیوانه is the noun 'the madman'. خندد is a present/characteristic 3rd-person form 'laughs' (classical/poetic present). ز is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'because of / at', and کردار خود = 'his own deed(s)', where خود is reflexive/possessive 'own' referring back to the subject.
segments.s250-g141: چه گونه is an interrogative phrase 'in what way / how'. گشادی is a noun/abstract 'openness / width' describing the opening. بخنده is an adverbial/gerundive form 'in laughter' (بـ- prefix + خنده), and دو لب 'the two lips' is the affected body-part phrase; word order is poetic but means 'how wide were the two lips opened in laughter'.
segments.s250-g142: که here introduces the resulting/perceptual clause: شب روز بینی literally 'night [as] day you see' where بینی is the present 2nd-person form 'you see' (classical usage). همی 'always/continually' modifies the following phrase روز شب 'day [as] night' — the line uses parallel juxtaposition (no explicit 'as' or را) typical of poetry to mean 'you see night as day and continually day as night.'
segments.s250-g143: چه رازست is a verbless/existential question with the archaic copula -ست 'is' meaning 'what is the secret?'. پیش آر is an imperative-like instruction: پیش ('before / forward') + آر (poetic imperative/verb meaning 'bring/show' or 'display'), so 'bring it forth'. و با من بگوی is 'and tell it to me' — بگوی here is a poetic 2nd-person imperative form of گفتن ('say / tell'), with با من 'to/with me'.
segments.s250-g144: مگر begins a rhetorical/suggestive clause here ('or perhaps / is it that...'). بخت نیکت is 'your good fortune' with the possessive enclitic -ت on نیک (nikt = 'your good/auspicious one'). نمودست is an archaic perfective/copular form meaning 'has appeared / has shown itself', and روی 'face' is the noun used idiomatically with نمودن: 'fortune has shown its face' = 'good fortune has appeared.'
segments.s250-g41: بدو گفت رستم is a short clause with the indirect object first: بدو (to him/her) + گفت (said) + رستم (Rostam). که‌ای زن چبود is direct speech introduced immediately after; که‌ای is a vocative-like address (literary = «که ای», “O …”), زن is the noun 'woman', and چبود (classical contraction of چه بود) asks 'what was/what happened'. The whole segment functions as 'Rostam said to her: “O woman, what happened?”'.
segments.s250-g42: مگر here introduces a rhetorical/expectant question ('could it be that…' / 'is it that…'). اهرمن is the proper name Ahriman (the devil). رستخیزت is 'رست‌خیز' + possessive suffix ـت ('your rising/uprising'), i.e. 'your being roused/uprising'. نمود is past tense of نمودن (to show/do/make) meaning here 'caused' or 'made (you) rise'. The segment is a single question: 'Could it be that Ahriman caused your uprising?'
segments.s250-g43: همی is an adverb of continuity ('always/continually'); بر here means 'upon/over'. نوشتی is used in the sense 'to tread down / trample' (an older meaning of the verbal root موجودِ نوشت/نورد in epic diction), تو is the second-person pronoun, بازار من is 'my bazaar/market' (possessive follows the noun in Persian). The sequence says: 'You have continually trodden upon my market.'
segments.s250-g44: بدان is here best read as a fused adverbial 'به + آن' / 'by that/for that' (hence 'because of that'); روی بد = 'bad/hostile face' i.e. hostile demeanor; با تو پیکار من places the preposition با 'with' before the object تو 'you' and then the noun پیکار 'battle/struggle' with the speaker's pronoun من after it for emphasis: 'my fight is with you' or 'my struggle is against you.' The whole clause explains the reason for hostility: 'Because of that hostile countenance, my fight is with you.'
segments.s250-g45: بدین تندی = 'with this harshness/violence' (بدین = 'in this way'); از من میازار بیش is an imperative admonition: میازار here functions as a prohibitive 'do not afflict/torment' with از من 'against me' and بیش 'anymore/further'. Note this is a literary/formal negative imperative construction (classical usage).
segments.s250-g46: که introduces the reason ('for/because'); دل‌بسته بودم is past-tense 'I was attached/enamored/devoted' (دل‌بسته = attached, بودم = I was). ببازار خویش = 'to/for my bazaar' (ببازار = 'to the market/in public' here 'to the bazaar' and خویش (split as خوی + ش in the word list) yields the possessive sense 'own/my'). The whole clause gives the motive: 'because I was attached to my bazaar.'
segments.s250-g47: و دیگر is a connective 'and besides/again/anymore'; بجایی is the locative notion 'to/at the place' (literary usage), and که introduces the relative clause 'where/that': کیخسروست is 'is Kay Khosrow' or more naturally 'where Kay Khosrow is'. So this first segment sets up a locative: 'and furthermore, to the place where Kay Khosrow is…'.
segments.s250-g48: بدان شهر = 'in/at that city' (بدان = به + آن, a fused demonstrative locative), من خود 'I myself' for emphasis, ندارم is present-tense negated 'I do not have' (here idiomatically 'I have no [place/seat]'), and نشست is 'seat/sitting' or 'a place to sit/settle'. Together: 'As for that city, I myself have no seat there' — i.e. I do not sit/settle there (no place/standing for me there).
segments.s250-g121: بدان is analyzable as the prepositional prefix بِ (meaning 'to/into') plus a demonstrative element; here it functions like 'to/into that'. چاه is the noun 'pit/well' and نزدیک is a postpositive adjective 'near' modifying چاه. آن بسته is a determiner + adjective phrase 'that (one) closed', and بر is a preposition ('on/upon, toward') that links this phrase to what follows.
segments.s250-g122: دگر هرچ is a phrase 'whatever else' (دگر = 'other/else' + هرچ = 'whatever'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', governing the following verb. ببر is the imperative/verb 'take!' (here used as the action commanded) and سربسر is an adverb 'completely/entirely' modifying ببر.
segments.s250-g123: بگسترد is a past/punctual verb 'he spread (out)' (ب- prefixed classical form). The proper name بیژن is the subject. پس is an adverb 'then' introducing the time/sequence, and آن نان پاک is the direct object 'that pure bread' (آن determiner + نان 'bread' + پاک 'pure').
segments.s250-g124: پراومید is a predicative/adjectival form 'full of hope' or 'hopeful' that here qualifies the state of the heart. یزدان is 'God' (object/source of hope), and دل is 'heart' (the thing affected). از introduces cause or removal 'from/because of', followed by بیم و باک 'fear and dread'. The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: literally 'hopeful (in) God the heart from fear and dread' = 'his heart, hopeful in God, from fear and dread.'
segments.s250-g125: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دست is the noun 'hand' and خورش is 'food/stew' (here the object toward which the hand moves). برد here is the past tense of بردن 'took/reached' (verb sense not listed among the provided senses, so marked -1). زان is a fused prepositional + demonstrative 'from that', and داوری is 'that judgment/matter' (the reason/occasion referred to). The whole phrase gives a temporal clause 'when his hand reached into the food because of that matter'.
segments.s250-g126: بدید is the past tense 'he saw'. آن نهان کرده انگشتری is a noun phrase 'that ring (which had been) hidden' where نهان کرده is a perfect participial modifier 'hidden/made hidden' (کرده as the participle) qualifying انگشتری 'ring'.
segments.s250-g127: نگینش is the noun 'his gem/signet' (نگین + possessive -ش). نگه کرد is the compound verb 'kept/held' (نگه as 'keeping' with کرد as the auxiliary/past verb). و is 'and'. نامش بخواند is 'he read/recited its name' (نام + possessive -ش, بخواند = he read/recited). The verb بخواند is used here in the narrative past sense 'he read'.
segments.s250-g128: ز شادی is a causal/prepositional phrase 'from/out of joy'. بخندید is the past tense 'he laughed'. و is 'and'. خیره بماند is a predicate 'remained staring' (خیره = staring/astonished used adjectivally, بماند = remained) — the adjective + بماند form describes his continued state.
segments.s250-g233: تو‌ای is a vocative address (poetic 'thou' / 'O you'); دخت is the head noun 'maiden/daughter' and is followed by the adjective phrase رنج آزموده (literally 'sorrow‑tried'), where آزموده is a past participle used adjectivally. The prepositional phrase ز من (= 'because of me / on my account') gives the cause of her suffering.
segments.s250-g234: فدا کرده is a light verbal construction: فدا (sacrifice/devotion) + کرده (past participle/auxiliary) yields 'has sacrificed/devoted'. The nouns جان، دل، چیز، تن are a coordinated list of the things sacrificed; و is the simple conjunction 'and'.
segments.s250-g235: بدین is the fused form 'به + این' meaning 'by/with this' (here glossed 'by this'); رنج is 'suffering'; کز (ک + ز) is a relative/causal particle 'that/which / from which' and with من gives 'that/which is from me / on my account'; برداشتی is 2nd person past (you took/you bore/you assumed). The clause means 'by this suffering which you bore because of me'.
segments.s250-g236: This short clause has the pattern 'زیان مرا سود پنداشتی' = 'you considered my زیان (loss) to be سود (profit)'. پنداشتی is past 'you thought/considered'. م + را in the token list is effectively مرا ('me/my') where را is the object/definiteness marker attached to the clitic; syntactically زیان is the noun being evaluated and سود is the predicate complement.
segments.s250-g237: بدادی is the past 2nd‑person form of دادن ('you gave' / archaic 'thou gavest'); بمن = به + من 'to me'. The nouns گنج، تاج، گهر form a coordinated list of direct objects: 'treasure, crown, jewel(s)'.
segments.s250-g238: This continues the list (or names further recipients/objects): جهاندار 'the world‑ruler / sovereign' and then a coordinated series خویشان و مام و پدر 'relatives and (dialectal) mother and father'. In classical Persian long lists often follow the verb without repeating it; مام is a dialectal/archaic word for 'mother'.
segments.s250-g239: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). یابم here is the 1st‑person subjunctive/optative form of یافتن (grammatical sense 'may I find / if I can get'); از چنگ = 'from the claw/grip of' (چنگ used metaphorically as 'clutch'); این اژدها = 'this dragon'. The clause means 'if I can get [someone/it] out of this dragon's grip'.
segments.s250-g240: بدین is again 'به + این' ('in/at this'); روزگار 'time, age' => 'at this time'; جوانی is 'youth / the young man' and رها is 'free / set free' (here adjectival/participial or resultative). The phrase functions as the consequent: '(then) I will free the youth at this time' — note classical Persian often omits an explicit auxiliary, using participles or verbless clauses.
segments.s250-g241: This is a nominal phrase listing qualities: بکردار is an adjective-like form meaning 'virtuous/of good conduct' placed before the plural noun نیکان 'the good (ones)'; یزدان‌پرست is an appositive noun 'God‑worshipper / pious person' further describing them. The sequence names the people the speaker aligns with ('the virtuous, the God‑worshippers').
segments.s250-g242: This line contains three coordinated verbs with the old prefix ب‑ marking present/optative/imperative action from the speaker: بپویم 'I will pursue/seek' (first‑person sense of پوییدن), بپای 'I will lie in wait / stand watch' (پاییدن), and بیازم 'I will break forth / burst out' (here an active, forceful motion). بدست is an adverbial phrase 'with (my) hand / in hand' modifying بیازم; word order places the instrument after the verbs.
segments.s250-g243: بسان 'like/as' introduces a simile. پرستار here means a devoted/worshipful attendant (used as 'devotee' or respectful petitioner) and پیش کیان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of the kings' (پیش = 'before/in front of', کیان = 'kings'). The whole phrase = 'as one who bows/devotes himself before the kings.'
segments.s250-g244: This clause states the speaker's action: بپاداش 'as a reward' sets the intended purpose/role; نیکیت is a possessive or object form 'your goodness/virtue' (نیکی + -ت); بندم is 1st‑person present/future of بستن here 'I will bind/place'; میان 'between / in the midst' marks location. Syntactically it says 'I will place/bind (something) amid (as) a reward for your goodness.'
segments.s250-g245: منیژه is the proper name (subject). به + هیزم marks direction 'toward / to the firewood.' شتابید is the simple past 'she hurried.' سخت functions as an adverbial intensifier 'greatly / forcefully.' Word order is subject – directional phrase – verb – adverbial intensifier.
segments.s250-g246: چو here means 'when.' مرغان (plural) is the subject 'the birds.' برآمد is the past 'arose / flew up / rose.' بشاخ is a prepositional compound 'on the branch' and درخت 'tree' names whose branch it is; the phrase gives the scene 'when the birds rose onto the tree‑branch.'
segments.s250-g247: بخورشید here is best read as به خورشید 'toward the sun' (the fused form marks direction). بر چشم is a locative phrase 'to the eye / to the sight' (literally 'upon the eye'), and هیزم ببر contains the imperative ببر 'take! / carry!' directing someone to take the firewood (هیزم). The clause contains two coordinated imperatives/directions: turn toward the sun (so you can see) and carry the wood.
segments.s250-g248: This is an exclamatory/interrogative clause introduced by که تا کی 'for how long': که functions here as a clause introducer, تا کی = 'until when / how long.' برآرد is a present/future‑oriented verb 'will raise/bring forth' (here 'raise'), شب 'night', از 'from', کوه 'mountain', سر 'head/top' — together: 'for how long will the night raise its head from the mountain?'
segments.s250-g1: This is a short clause with subject + verb + object/complement: منیژه (proper name) is the subject, خبر یافت is a verbal phrase meaning 'found/learned news' (یافت is past tense 'found/obtained'), and از کاروان specifies the source of the news ('about/from the caravan'). The preposition از links کاروان as the origin of the report.
segments.s250-g2: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn' modifying the following action; بشهر اندر آمد is an archaic word order meaning 'came into the city' (بشهر = بهِ شهر, اندر = into/in), and دوان (a verbal/adverbial word meaning 'running/rushing') qualifies the manner, so the whole phrase means 'they ran into the city one by one.' The plural subject is understood from context.
segments.s250-g3: This is a chain of modifiers before the noun: برهنه ('naked/exposed') and نوان ('young/youthful') qualify دخت ('daughter/maiden'), followed by the possessor افراسیاب. Classical/poetic Persian commonly places adjectives before the noun and omits an explicit ezāfe, so the phrase reads 'the naked, young daughter of Afrasiab.'
segments.s250-g4: بر رستم آمد is literally 'came toward Rostam' (بر + proper name + آمد). The trailing nominal phrase دو دیده پر آب is an adverbial/ablative description 'with two eyes full of water' (a poetic way to say 'with tear-filled eyes'); دو دیده = 'both eyes', پر = 'full (of)', آب = 'water/tears'.
segments.s250-g5: برو آفرین کرد is an idiomatic praise: آفرین کرد = 'he praised / exclaimed praise' and برو here functions as an interjection (part of the exclamation). The coordination و پرسید و گفت links two successive actions 'and he asked, and he said' (pursuing the dialogue). Verbs are past tense narrative (کرد, پرسید) and listed in sequence.
segments.s250-g6: همی is an archaic/poetic particle (here functioning as a connective/continuative 'then' or 'meanwhile'); به آستین introduces the goal/target 'onto (his) sleeve' (به = to/onto, آستین = sleeve). خون مژگان literally 'the blood of the eyelashes' is a poetic image (tears described as 'blood'); برفت is a past action 'went upon/flowed onto' — together the clause means 'the blood/tears of her eyelashes flowed onto his sleeve.'
segments.s250-g7: که introduces a wish or result clause ('that' / 'may it be that'); برخوردی here is used in a jussive/optative nuance meaning 'you may meet/suffer' or 'a striking event' (poetic/archaic); از جان وز گنج خویش lists the things affected: از جان = 'from your life' (i.e. loss of life), وز = an archaic fused particle meaning 'and/of' linking to گنج خویش = 'your treasure' (خویش split as 'خوی' + 'ش' = 'own' + possessive). The whole segment expresses danger to life and treasure.
segments.s250-g8: مبادت is an optative/negative-jussive formula 'may it not be' (used to express 'may ... not happen'); پشیمانی 'regret' is the thing wished away, and از رنج خویش ('from your suffering') gives the cause/source. Together: 'may regret not come as a result of your suffering.' Note the classical word order and the poetic negative wish marker.
segments.s250-g193: بگویش is the imperative بگو plus the enclitic ـش (to him); it means “tell him.” که is the conjunction “that” introducing the content clause. آری is an emphatic affirmation (“indeed/yes”). خداوند is the subject noun (here ‘the Lord/God’). رخش is the name of the steed (a direct object in the overall clause).
segments.s250-g194: ترا is written as ت + را here: ت is the 2nd-person pronominal element ‘to/you’ and را is the definite object marker; together تو را = “you” as indirect/affected object. داد is the past verb “gave.” یزدان is another word for God (subject) and فریاد بخش is an epithet in apposition (a compound idea: فریاد = “cry” and بخش used as ‘grant/giver’), so the phrase reads ‘God, the giver in response to cries’ — i.e. God gave (it) to you.
segments.s250-g195: ز here is the poetic equivalent of از ‘from.’ زاول (زاول) means ‘from the outset/first,’ and بایران (here used like a place-name or epithet of a region in the epic) names the region (treated as an adverbial/place). The sequence is a string of locative/original items introduced by ز (‘from’).
segments.s250-g196: ز بهر means ‘for the sake of’ (ز + بهر, an older idiom). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun ‘you.’ پیمودم is the 1st-person past singular of پیمودن ‘I traversed/covered’ (پیمود + ـم). این راه دور is the direct object phrase ‘this distant road/path.’
segments.s250-g197: بگویش (imperative ‘tell him’) introduces another reported clause with که ‘that.’ ما را uses ما plus the object marker را (we/us as the affected party). بسان is the comparative preposition ‘like/as,’ and پلنگ is the simile’s noun ‘leopard/panther.’
segments.s250-g198: بسود is an adverbial/phrase meaning ‘to the benefit/advantage (of).’ از پی تو literally ‘in pursuit of you’ (از پی = following/after; تو = you). کمرگاه و چنگ are two nouns joined by و: کمرگاه = ‘waist / small of the back’ and چنگ = ‘claw/talon’; they name the places/means of the attack or the parts involved.
segments.s250-g199: چو is the classical temporal/conditional conjunction ‘when’ (or ‘if’). با او بگویی uses با + او and the subjunctive/epistolary form بگویی (‘you speak/say’ in dependent clause after چو). سخن راز دار is an instruction: سخن ‘speech/words’ + راز دار (‘secret-holding’/‘keep secret’); دار here functions as the bound element meaning ‘having/holding’ (i.e., ‘keep it secret’), so the whole clause means ‘when you speak with him, keep the talk secret.’
segments.s250-g200: شب تیره is an adjectival phrase ‘the dark night.’ گوشت به آواز دار is an idiomatic instruction: گوشت (here used in the sense of attending/listening) + به ‘to’ + آواز ‘voice/sound’ + دار (again ‘hold/keep’ as a bound element) — together ‘lend your ear to the sound’ or ‘attend to the voice.’ The clause is a parallel admonition to the previous one.
segments.s250-g89: مگر here functions as 'unless' (a conditional particle). بر تو places the action toward you ('brings/for you'), with تو the 2nd-person pronoun. بخشایش آرد is an archaic/poetic order: بخشایش (a noun 'forgiveness') plus آرد (3rd-person singular present/present-subjunctive of آوردن used poetically as 'brings' or 'grants'); پدر is the agent 'the father'.
segments.s250-g90: بجوشدش is a verb form meaning 'may it boil / may it seethe' with the enclitic -ش = 'his' attached (so 'his blood may boil'); this is a jussive/subjunctive wish. خون is the noun 'blood' (the subject of the jussive). و simply connects the two wishes. بسوزد is another jussive/subjunctive 'may ... burn' and جگر ('jigar') is used as the internal organ/seat of feeling (heart/liver) that 'burns' — an idiomatic expression of grief or anger.
segments.s250-g91: گر introduces a conditional 'if'. آزار بابت = 'harm on account of' (آزار 'harm', بابت 'on account of/for'). نبودی is the past negative form 'there had not been' (past-tense existence/non-occurrence); ز پیش ('from before') means 'earlier' or 'previously'.
segments.s250-g92: ترا is the dative/benefactive 'to you' composed of the old enclitic ت plus the object marker را (split in the annotation). دادمی is an archaic conditional/optative form 'I would give' (past-stem + -می used for counterfactual). چیز 'a thing' is the direct object; ز اندازه بیش literally 'from the measure more' = 'more than the proper measure' (ز = 'from/of', اندازه = 'measure/extent', بیش = 'more').
segments.s250-g93: بخوالیگرش appears as an adverbial or vocative phrase (likely naming or addressing a person/attendant) and functions as the addressee; گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. کز هر خورش = 'that/from every dish' (کز = 'that/from which', هر = 'each/every', خورش = 'dish/stew'). Together this introduces a content clause describing what was said.
segments.s250-g94: که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/which'. او را is the patient/object 'him' (او + object marker را). بباید here is a modal 'must/ought to' (used with a following verb in classical Persian). بیاور is the present-subjunctive/imperative stem 'bring' (here used as the action required), and برش is a clitic phrase meaning 'to him/for him' (directional/clitic -ش); the whole clause instructs that from every dish that should be brought, it should be brought to him.
segments.s250-g95: یکی 'one/a certain' introduces the item. مرغ بریان is the noun plus adjective 'roasted chicken' (object), and بفرمود is an honorific/preterite verb 'he ordered/commanded/said' (literally 'he said' used as a polite/royal verb for ordering). گرم follows as a postposed adjective 'hot' describing the chicken.
segments.s250-g96: نوشته here is a noun or verbal noun 'a writing/note' (literally 'a written [thing]'). بدو = 'to him' (be + 3rd-person enclitic), so نوشته بدو = 'a note addressed to him' or 'he wrote to him'. اندرون + نان نرم gives the content/instruction: 'inside, soft bread' — i.e. the note reads 'soft bread inside'. اندرون functions as a locative 'inside' modifying نان نرم 'soft bread'.
segments.s250-g177: ببخشود is an archaic/perfective verb form here used optatively ('may (he) grant' / 'granting'), followed by the prepositional phrase بر من meaning 'to me' (بر + من = 'for/to me'). جهان‌آفرین is a vocative/appositive title 'O world‑creator / the Creator' addressed as the agent of the verb. Word order is verb + indirect object + vocative, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s250-g178: ببینم is a first‑person subjunctive/optative ('that I may see' / 'let me see'); مگر here functions as a moderating particle ('perhaps' / 'maybe' in context); پهن روی زمین is a noun phrase 'the broad surface/face of the earth' with پهن 'broad' modifying روی 'surface/face' and زمین 'earth' as the head noun.
segments.s250-g179: رهاند is a third‑person form used optatively/imperatively here ('may he free/deliver'). The object مرا is split orthographically into م + را (م = 'me', را = definite/object marker), so the clause means 'free me'. زین غمان دراز is an old/poetic expression 'from this long sorrow' (زین = 'from this' in classical usage, غمان = 'sorrows', دراز = 'long').
segments.s250-g180: This clause continues the verb's application to a second object: ترا (ت + را = 'you' + object marker) is 'you' as the object. زین تکاپوی و گرم و گداز = 'from this turmoil/bustle and from heat and burning anguish' — تکاپوی 'struggle/bustle', گرم 'hot/fierce', گداز 'burning/ardor' (metaphorical for anguish). The conjunction و links the modifiers; the verb from the previous segment is understood to govern this object as well.
segments.s250-g181: بنزدیک is an imperative/adverbial 'draw near' or 'come close'; او is the object pronoun 'to him'; شو is an imperative auxiliary 'become' (here forming the command 'become near him' = 'approach him'); بگویش is an imperative 'tell' with enclitic ـش ('to him'); نهان is an adverb 'secretly' modifying the speech act. The sequence gives two commands: approach him and tell him secretly.
segments.s250-g182: که‌ای is an interrogative/vocative combination here ('who are you?' or 'who is it? / who art thou?'), followed by پهلوان 'champion/hero' and the attributive genitive kiان جهان 'of the Kian world' (کیان = the royal/Kian house; جهان = world). The whole is the secret question addressed to the person approached: 'Who are you, O hero of the Kian world?'
segments.s250-g183: بدل here means 'in return / recompense' (an instruction to give something in return), مهربان qualifies it as 'kindly'. The conjunction و links to بتن چاره جوی where بتن is the contracted phrase به تن 'to (his) body' and چاره جوی is an imperative 'seek a remedy' (جوی 'seek/search' modifying چاره 'remedy'). Together this gives two imperatives: repay kindly and look for a cure for his body.
segments.s250-g184: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'; تو is the pronoun 'you'; خداوند here is a title 'lord/master' (addressing someone as master); رخشی is a reference to رخش (Rakhsh, the horse) in an attributive form ('of Rakhsh' / 'Rakhsh‑master'); بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'say' or 'speak'. Grammatically this is a protasis + admonition: 'If you are the master of Rakhsh, then speak/say (it).' The copula in the nominal condition is omitted as is common in Persian.
segments.s250-g145: بدو is a prepositional/directional clitic meaning "to him/to her/to that one" placed before the verb; گفت is the main verb "said" (the provided senses don't include the verbal sense, so treat it as the speech verb). بیژن is the subject (name) that follows the verb in this inverted/poetic SOV order. کزین = کِ + این (archaic contraction) means "from this" and modifies the following noun کارسخت "this hard task/ordeal."
segments.s250-g146: بر + اومید functions as the idiom بر اومید "I hope" (literally 'upon hope'); آنم که introduces a subordinate clause "that ..." (آنم here = 'that I / that which'). بگشاد is an archaic/poetic prefixed verbal form (ب- + گشاد) expressing the idea "may open / will open"; it takes بخت "fortune/luck" as its object.
segments.s250-g147: چو at clause-initial position here = "if" (conditional). با من "with me / to me" marks the addressee of the promised oath. بسوگند is an adverbial/phrasing "by oath / sworn" and پیمان کنی uses کنی (subjunctive/present 2nd-person) from کردن meaning "you make/enter (a pact)." The clause means "if you swear a pledge to me."
segments.s250-g148: همانا is an intensifier 'verily/truly'. وفای مرا is a genitive/object phrase ('my fidelity' or 'fidelity to me'); م here is the clitic for 'me' attached before the object marker را (the dataset's lemma for م doesn't match its function, so senses are limited). نشکنی is the negative subjunctive/optative 2nd-person form (نَ + شکنی) meaning 'you will not break' or 'may you not break (it)'.
segments.s250-g149: بگویم is 1st-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'I will tell / let me tell' used here as a simple statement of intent. سراسر 'throughout/entire' modifies داستان 'story'; تورا is the object pronoun 'to you' placed before the noun for emphasis/poetic word order: 'I will tell you the whole tale.' (Note: the provided senses for بگویم are nominal and do not include the verbal sense, so that sense is reported as unavailable.)
segments.s250-g150: چو here is conditional 'if/when'. باشی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive of بودن 'you are / you be' and introduces the condition. بسوگند 'by oath' qualifies the temporal/conditional clause ('if you, having sworn / when you swear'). همداستان is a nominal predicate meaning 'like-minded / of the same mind' — together: 'if when you swear you are of the same mind.'
segments.s250-g151: که‌گر (که گر fused) is an archaic/poetic conditional 'if'. لب is the direct object 'lip(s)'. بدوزی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive (or hortative/conditional) of دوختن 'you sew (shut)'; the prefixed ب- is the classical verbal prefix. ز بهر گزند is an archaic prepositional phrase 'for the sake of / because of harm' (ز بهر = 'for / on account of', گزند = 'harm').
segments.s250-g152: زنان را زبان is a genitive/possessive object phrase 'the tongues of women' with را marking definiteness/object. کم بماند uses کم 'little/insufficient' + بماند (present/subjunctive of ماندن) meaning 'will remain little / will still be (to a small degree)'. The final ببند is an imperative 'shut (it)!' or 'be silent!'; it addresses the addressee and closes the admonition. The line as a whole says: even if you sew your mouth shut against harm, women's tongues will persist — so shut (yours) up!
segments.s250-g97: سبک دست رستم is a descriptive phrase: سبکِ دست = 'light-/nimble-handed' (adjective + noun) with رستم as the possessor (Rostam). بسان introduces a simile 'like/as', and پری supplies the comparison 'a fairy'. Word order (adjective + noun + possessor + simile) is poetic but means 'Rostam’s hand, light as a fairy's.'
segments.s250-g98: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object). درنهان here functions as an adverbial phrase 'dar nekhān' = 'in secret/secretly' (poetic fused form). کرد is simple past 3rd-sg 'he did/placed', and انگشتری is the direct object 'a ring'. The clause order is somewhat compressed: 'to him secretly placed (a) ring.'
segments.s250-g99: بدو داد = 'he gave to him' (داد past 3rd-sg with indirect-object phrase بدو). و گفتش = 'and he said to him' (گفت + enclitic ش = 'to him'). بدان is here a directional/demonstrative combination meaning 'to that' (بَ + آن) and چاه بر adds 'to/ward that well' (بر as directional 'toward/to'); the whole segment frames what he handed and the direction/target he mentioned.
segments.s250-g100: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'because' / 'that'). بیچارگان را is the definite/plural object 'the wretched/the unfortunate' marked by را. توی is a locative/prepositional 'among/in' and راهبر is 'guide/leader' — here the clause is elliptical as reported speech attached to گفتش: 'for you are (to be) the guide of the wretched,' with the copula/imperative implied.
