segments.s250-g101: منیژه is the subject (proper name). بیامد is past 'came' (3rd-sg). بدان is directional 'to that' (ب + آن) and چاه سر (سرِ چاه) = 'the mouth/edge of the well' (سر = 'top/edge') — together: 'Manijeh came to the mouth of that well.'
segments.s250-g102: دوان is a past/action verb 'ran/rushed' (narrative simple past). و = 'and'. خورشها is the plural 'the stews/cooked dishes'. گرفته is a perfect participle 'having seized/held' (or 'seized') and ببر here functions as a verb meaning 'carry/take away' (an older narrative use: 'he bore off/removed' rather than a literal imperative). The sequence gives two quick actions: she ran, seized the food and carried it off.
segments.s250-g103: نوشته is a noun 'the written text/letter'. بدستار qualifies it as 'of the turbaned one' (بدستار = 'turbaned' or 'with a turbān'), so 'the turbaned man's writing' or 'the writing of the turbaned man'. چیزی که برد is a relative clause 'the thing which he had taken' (که = 'which/that'; برد here is past 'took/removed' in context).
segments.s250-g104: چنان هم که = 'just so/just as', introducing the manner clause. بستد is past 'he closed/sealed/tied' (3rd-sg). ببیژن is the indirect object 'to Bijan' (ب + بیژن, dative). سپرد is the main past verb 'he entrusted/handed over'. The clause order is: manner clause ('just as he sealed it') followed by the action 'he entrusted it to Bijan.'
segments.s250-g169: چنین گفتم is a prepositional/ adverbial + verb phrase meaning “I said thus” (چنین = thus; گفتم = 1st‑person past of گفتن). اکنون نبایست گفت is an impersonal modal clause: اکنون = now; نبایست (archaic/poetic form of نباید/نبایَد) = ‘ought not / should not’; گفت here functions as the verbal root/infinitive ‘to say’ (classical texts often use the past stem as an infinitive). Together: “I said thus: now one ought not to say/ask….”
segments.s250-g170: ایا introduces an indirect question or whether‑clause (‘whether’). مهربان یار و هشیار جفت is a coordinated noun phrase: مهربان یار = ‘kind/merciful companion’; و links the second conjunct; هشیار جفت = ‘a watchful/alert spouse or mate’ (جفت = mate/spouse). The full clause asks whether he is both a kind companion and a prudent/awake partner.
segments.s250-g171: سزد‌گر is a conditional/concessive opening meaning ‘if it is fitting’ or ‘if one deems it right’ (سزد + گر). بهر کار = ‘for the matter/for the affair’ (بهر = for; کار = affair). پندم دهی = ‘you give my counsel’ — پندم = پند + possessive ـم (‘my advice’); دهی = 2nd‑person present/subjunctive/imperative form of دادن ‘you give’. The order places the benefactive phrase and object before the verb, typical in Persian.
segments.s250-g172: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause here (‘for / because’). مغزم = مغز + ـم (‘my brain/mind’) as subject. برنج اندرون شد تهی is a predication: برنج اندرون = ‘rice inside/within the interior’ (literal wording); شد = past auxiliary ‘became’; تهی = ‘empty’. Grammatically this reads literally ‘my brain became empty of rice within,’ i.e. the brain/mind has been emptied (metaphorically).
segments.s250-g173: تو بشناس is an imperative/imperatival jussive address: تو = ‘you’ (emphatic); بشناس = present/imperfect imperative of شناختن ‘know/recognize’ (command or heed). کاین مرد گوهر فروش = ‘that this man, a gem/jewel‑seller’ (کاین = this/that; مرد = man; گوهر فروش = jeweler/gem‑seller) — a standard ‘know that …’ construction.
segments.s250-g174: که begins a relative clause modifying مرد گوهر فروش (‘who / that’). خوالیگرش is an opaque/archaic noun plus 3rd‑person possessive suffix ـش (‘his/its’); its exact lexical meaning is obscure here. The sequence مر ترا داد توش contains clitic object pronouns and the verb in classical order: مر = me (accusative clitic); ت + را = ترا (to you / second‑person object with the definite marker); داد = gave (past 3rd‑person); توش = ‘in it / therein’ (adverbial locative with clitic). So the clause literally says that his خوالیگر “gave me to you in it” (wording and exact referents are context‑dependent).
segments.s250-g175: ز بهر من = ‘for my sake’ (ز here is the poetic form of از/for/from and with بهر ‘for’ gives the purposive ‘for the sake of’); آمد = past ‘he came’; بتوران = به + توران ‘to Turan’; فراز = adverb ‘up / on high / upward’. The phrase orders the purposive prepositional phrase before the verb, then the destination and accompanying adverb.
segments.s250-g176: وگرنه = ‘otherwise’. نبودش is the past negative with clitic ـش referring back to the person (‘he would not be / there would not be in him’). بگوهر نیاز groups a prepositional/qualifying element with noun: بگوهر = ‘with/of jewels’ (classical form meaning ‘of gems’); نیاز = ‘need/necessity’. Together the clause means ‘otherwise he would not have need of jewels’ (i.e. he came for my sake; otherwise he wouldn’t care for jewels).
segments.s250-g113: This short phrase strings prepositional elements in a poetic order: از ایران بتوران = 'from Iran to Turan' (از ... بتوران). The following ز بهر درم is a fused purpose/cause phrase meaning 'for the sake of a dirham' (ز = 'because of', بهر = 'for', درم = 'dirham').
segments.s250-g114: کشیده is a past participle (from کشیدن) used here in the sense 'has endured / has drawn' and takes ز هر گونه 'from/of every kind' as its complement; بسیار modifies غم ('very much sorrow'). The particle ز before هر is the poetic prepositional use 'from/of'.
segments.s250-g115: This noun phrase describes the man: یکی مرد پاکیزه = 'a certain/one man who was pure'; با + nouns (با هوش و فر) functions adjectivally to indicate possession/quality ('with intelligence and (royal) glory'). Note the adjective پاکیزه follows the noun in this poetic order.
segments.s250-g116: This is an adjunct giving what he had: ز هر گونه = 'of every kind'; با او = 'with him' (possession); فراوان modifies گهر, so the whole reads 'with him abundant gems of every kind.' Word order is inverted for poetic effect (possession phrase + adjective + noun).
segments.s250-g117: This clause uses نهاده (past participle of نهادن) meaning 'had set/established' with دستگاهی as its object (an 'establishment' or 'apparatus') and فراخ as an adjective 'spacious.' گشن appears as a modifier (literally 'hunger'/'for the hungry') used poetically to qualify the دستگاهی. The overall sense is 'he had established a broad/spacious provision/household.'
segments.s250-g118: Simple verbless nominal clause: یکی کلبه سازیده = 'he had made/built a small cottage'; بر پیش کاخ = 'before/in front of the palace' where بر + پیش gives the spatial relation 'in front of.' سازیده is a perfect participle used adjectivally with کلبه.
segments.s250-g119: بمن داد = 'he gave to me' (بمن = to me, داد = gave). زین گونه here is a fixed poetic expression 'of this kind / such a' (زین in the text functions demonstratively, though the supplied lemma senses do not list that use). دستارخوان is the noun given 'a ritual chanter / cleric.'
segments.s250-g120: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that / in order that' (here sense 'so that'); بر من = 'for me/on my behalf'; جهان‌آفرین را is the definite object ('the Creator') marked by را and placed before the verb; بخوان is subjunctive/imperative-like here meaning 'may he recite' or 'to recite.'
segments.s250-g105: نگه کرد is a light verbal sequence: the noun نگه (‘a gaze/keeping’) + the past auxiliary کرد (‘did’), giving ‘looked/kept watch’. The proper name بیژن follows the verb (inverted order common in epic Persian) and is the subject. بخیره is an adverb ‘safely/fortunately’ modifying the following verb بماند, which is 3rd‑person singular (imperfective/continuative form used here in narrative sense) meaning ‘remained’ or ‘was left’. Together: “Bijan looked/kept watch and remained safe.”
segments.s250-g106: ازان is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘from that’ (i.e., from that place). چاه is the place noun ‘well/shaft’. خورشید رخ is a compound epithet: خورشید (‘sun’) + رخ (‘face’) = ‘sun‑faced one’ (an epithet / addressee). را is the definite/object marker attached to the direct object (here marking رخ). بخواند is the past/stative form of خواندن used here in the sense ‘to call/summon; to cry out’. Word order is prepositional phrase + object + verb: ‘From that well he called to the sun‑faced (one).’
segments.s250-g107: که‌ای here is the poetic/vocative construction equivalent to ای که (‘O you who…’); مهربان is the vocative adjective ‘kind/merciful’. از introduces the interrogative phrase ‘from/where’, کجا is the interrogative ‘where’, and یافتی is 2nd‑person singular past of یافتن (‘to find’). Together this makes the address and question: “O kind one, where did you find…?”
segments.s250-g108: خورشها is the noun here (in the manuscript/form given) meaning ‘dishes/food’ (خورش). کزین is a fused prepositional/relative particle ک+زین ‘that/from this’, introducing the clause modifying خورشها. گونه is ‘manner/type’ and together with بشتافتی (2nd‑person singular past of بشتافتن ‘to hasten’) yields the relative clause ‘that you hastened in this manner’ (i.e., ‘which you brought so quickly’).
segments.s250-g109: بسا is an adverb meaning ‘many/often’; here it quantifies the following nouns. رنج و سختی are a pair of nouns ‘suffering and hardship’. کت (literally ‘coat’) is used figuratively: آمد بروی is a verbal phrase meaning ‘came upon you’ (آمد ‘came’ + بروی indicating direction/target 'to you' or 'upon you'); the clause means ‘many sufferings and hardships came upon you (as if a coat fell on you)’. Note that classical Persian often uses such imagery with کت.
segments.s250-g110: ز بهر منی is a purpose/benefactive phrase ‘for my sake’ (ز/بهر = ‘for’; من = ‘me/I’; the final ی is a poetic or enclitic element here bound to the phrase). در جهان = ‘in the world’. پوی پوی is a reduplicated adjective/verb form meaning ‘roving/rovingly’ or ‘constantly seeking’ (repetition intensifies the sense). Overall: ‘for my sake you roam the world, seeking.’
segments.s250-g111: منیژه is the speaker’s name (Manijeh). بدو is the clitic phrase ‘to him / unto him’ (بد + او). گفت is the verb ‘said’ (past); کز here is the relative/prepositional particle meaning ‘that/from which’ and links to what follows (‘from the caravan’). The segment functions as ‘Manijeh said to him that/from the caravan…’.
segments.s250-g112: یکی is an indefinite ‘a certain / someone’. مایه ور in classical Persian works as a compound meaning ‘possessing resources’ (hence ‘wealthy’ or ‘rich’); here it modifies مرد بازارگان ‘man/merchant’. مرد بازارگان is a noun phrase ‘a merchant man’ = ‘a merchant’. The whole phrase therefore means ‘a certain wealthy merchant.’
segments.s250-g33: به رستم نگه کرد: a prepositional phrase + nominal object + compound verb. به + رستم marks direction/target ('toward Rostam'), نگه (a noun 'gaze') combines with کردن (کرد) to make the verbal phrase 'to cast a look' (literal 'made a look'). و بگریست زار: بگریست is simple past (3rd sg.) with the old verbal prefix ب- and means 'he wept'; زار is an adjective/adverb 'in wretched grief' used adverbially to qualify the weeping.
segments.s250-g34: ز خواری ببارید خون بر کنار: ز خواری is a causal/ablative phrase 'from/because of humiliation'; ببارید is past tense (literally 'it rained/fell') used with a liquid subject; here the subject (خون) follows the verb in inverted poetic order, so 'blood fell'. بر کنار marks the place/side 'upon/at the side'. The clause is poetic inversion (verb before subject) and uses archaic past forms.
segments.s250-g35: بدو گفت کای مهتر پرخرد: بدو is a dative adverbial 'to him' (he addressed him); گفت here functions as the verb 'said' (classical word order is pronoun/dative + verb). کای is the vocative 'O' and precedes the titles that follow; مهتر 'chief/distinguished one' and پرخرد 'full of wisdom' are epithets placed after the vocative as honorifics.
segments.s250-g36: ز تو سرد گفتن نه اندر خورد: ز تو سرد گفتن is an abstract/infinitival phrase 'speaking coldly of you' (ز + pronoun + adjective + verbal noun); سرد is figurative 'aloof/harsh.' نه اندر خورد is an idiomatic negative: اندر 'in' + خورد (here an archaic predicative meaning 'befit' or 'sit well with') — together 'does not befit / is not fitting.' Note that خورد carries an older/poetic sense 'to be fitting' rather than the literal 'eat.'
segments.s250-g37: سخن‌گر نگویی مرانم ز پیش: سخن‌گر 'speaker/one given to talk' is the predicate/name being rejected. نگویی is a negative present/subjunctive/imperative sense 'do not say / you should not call' addressed to the addressee. مرانم appears as an archaic pronominal form meaning roughly 'me' (object), i.e. 'do not call me'. ز پیش 'beforehand' or 'in the presence (of others)' qualifies the refusal. The segment uses compact, archaic/phrasal clitics and inverted order common in epic diction.
segments.s250-g38: که من خود دلی دارم از درد ریش: که is the conjunction 'for/because' introducing the explanation; من خود is emphatic 'I myself'; دلی دارم = 'I have a heart' (دلی here noun 'a heart' or 'feeling'); از درد introduces the source 'from the pain of ...'. ریش is a poetic/archaic noun in this line (literal 'beard' in the dictionary list, but here functioning in a figurative/poetic sense related to laceration/harrowed pain), so the whole clause explains that he is inwardly wounded by pain.
segments.s250-g39: چنین باشد آیین ایران مگر: چنین باشد is an optative/jussive phrasing 'so should it be' or 'let it be thus' (باشـد used in the sense 'ought to be'); آیین ایران 'the custom/way of Iran' is the subject/nominal phrase; مگر here introduces an exception or qualification 'unless' (setting up the following clause). The order is compact and typical of classical Persian.
segments.s250-g40: که درویش را کس نگوید خبر: که 'that' introduces the subordinate clause; درویش را uses را as the dative/definite object marker 'to the dervish' (indirect object); کس 'anyone' combines with the negated verb نگوید to produce 'no one tells' (negation scope); خبر is the thing told 'news'. Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before final object) as is common in epic diction; نگوید is an archaic/present subjunctive/negative form meaning 'does not tell' or 'will not tell'.
segments.s250-g81: بگویی is an optative/conditional second‑person form meaning roughly “you would say” or “you might say.” که introduces the content clause that follows. The subject بیژن comes after the complementizer (typical flexible Persian word order), while بسختی (an adverb) modifies the following predicate درست, where درست here functions as an intensifying predicative element meaning “truly/indeed” or “in a bad condition.”
segments.s250-g82: This is a protasis introduced by اگر (if). دیر (here) functions as ‘late / taking long’ and گیری (from گرفت/گیر) stands for ‘taking / getting’ (forming the idea of ‘delaying’); together دیر گیری = ‘a delay’ or ‘taking too long.’ شود is the impersonal/future/optative auxiliary ‘will happen / become.’ کار پست is the result clause (a noun phrase) meaning ‘a base/despicable deed’ that will come about; Persian often places the predicate (شود) before the noun phrase object in poetic prose.
segments.s250-g83: گرش here is a fused/poetic form (functionally equivalent to اگر + ش = “if him/if you see him” in older usage); دید is the verb ‘see.’ خواهی is the second‑person future/auxiliary ‘you will (wish),’ and میاسای is a verb meaning ‘to set at rest / to let rest’ (a subjunctive/optative meaning ‘you would cause to rest’). دیر in this clause is the noun ‘monastery’ (classical/poetic sense) and is the place where one would ‘let him rest.’ The sequence is conditional: “If you see him, you will want to set him to rest in a monastery.”
segments.s250-g84: که introduces a reason/explanatory clause (‘for/that’). بر سرش is a prepositional phrase ‘upon his head’ (بر + سر + enclitic ش = his). سنگست is a poetic adjective/phrase meaning ‘stone/rock (lies there)’ or ‘stony,’ used predicatively here; و links the second item آهن بزیر, where بزیر is an inverted poetic order meaning ‘beneath / under’ (آهن بزیر = ‘iron underneath’). The clause explains why one would wish to let him rest (because stone is on his head and iron beneath).
segments.s250-g85: بدو is the prepositional combination be + u = ‘to him’ (to/unto him). گفت is the verb ‘said’ (perfect/past) introducing direct speech. رستم is the subject/agent (word order here places the name after the verb, which is common in Persian). که‌ای خوب‌چهر is the opening of the quoted address: که (introducing the speech) + ای (vocative particle) + خوب‌چهر (vocative adjective ‘fair‑faced’).
segments.s250-g86: که again introduces the content of the speech (a wish). مهرت is the noun ‘your affection / favour.’ مبراد is an archaic/obscure form expressing a negative jussive/optative (here conveying ‘may it not be removed’); the exact morphology is old/poetic. از وی سپهر is an inverted prepositional phrase ‘by/from him (or by heaven)’ with سپهر (‘sky, heaven’) as the agent; the whole gives a wish: ‘may heaven not remove your affection from him.’
segments.s250-g87: چرا is the interrogative ‘why.’ نزد is a preposition meaning ‘near / in the presence of / at’ and takes باب تو (‘your gate/door’) as its object (نزد + باب + تو = ‘at your gate’). خواهشگران is the plural noun ‘supplicants’ or ‘petitioners’ and functions as the subject/topic of the question. The whole segment asks why petitioners are gathered at the addressee’s threshold.
segments.s250-g88: نینگیزی is an adjective meaning ‘shameful / disgraceful’ and here qualifies the supplicants (either in apposition or as an adjectival phrase). از هر سوی is a common prepositional phrase ‘from every side / from every quarter.’ مهتران is an older/poetic plural meaning ‘the great ones / chiefs’; the word order is compressed and poetic, so this segment describes shameful petitioners coming from all sides of the chiefs (or coming from chiefs on all sides).
